what_it_does: This file is the global CSS for the canvas app. It maps legacy app.css variable names to a central corpus design-system token set, defines the overall dark/warm-gold atmosphere, living-aurora animations, and a shared visual kit (section heads, lanes, badges, surfaces, empty states), then styles the tldraw canvas, node cards, HUD panels (toolbar, activity, palette, inspector, right-hand-man chat, inbox, build-intent cards, address help, settings), and interaction affordances.
observations: The :root block aliases legacy names like --panel, --ink, --sig, --warn, --err to corpus tokens such as --s1, --tx, --acc, --await, --fail.
The body background is built from layered radial gradients (warm gold upper-right, deep glow lower-left) over the base --bg, plus a fixed ::before aurora layer and a subtle ::after noise grain.
A `body.canvas-running` class intensifies the aurora (brighter, faster) to signal an in-flight run.
There is a large 'shared kit' section (.kit-*) defining reusable primitives: section heads, lane heads, badges, surfaces, and empty states, all token-tinted.
Node cards (.node-card) get border colors from corpus tokens per status: ran=--ok, cached=--cache, stuck/failed=--fail, live=--acc, empty=--line-2.
Idle nodes pulse during a run via `body.canvas-running .node-card.idle`.
A `.node-dot.ring` variant renders reference nodes as hollow rings instead of solid dots.
The toolbar, activity, palette, panel, and right-hand-man are absolute-positioned HUD surfaces using --s1, --line, --elev-1, etc.
The right-hand-man chat has a living presence orb (::before on .rhm-head) and a breathing keyframe.
Build-intent cards (.bi-*) encode lifecycle by left-border/phase color: inbox=amber, running=blue pulse, done=green, back=red.
Address help (.ahelp-*) uses a 'altitude' pattern: plain-language howto in display font, collapsed mechanism details.
A ripple-map SVG for blast-radius reach uses token-colored concentric rings.
Comments are dense and narrative, citing design waves, mockup sources, rule IDs (F1, U2, L10, G-25), and deleted/retired rules.
`@media (prefers-reduced-motion:reduce)` disables the aurora, entrance stagger, and breathing animations.
The file explicitly avoids hardcoded literals, deriving tints with `color-mix(in srgb, var(--X) Y%, transparent)`.
The provided file content is truncated near the end (cuts off inside `.br-ring-circle.br`).
standouts: The alias-layer at the top: legacy CSS variables are intentionally remapped to a single corpus token source rather than rewritten everywhere.
The 'living instrument' atmosphere: warm gold aurora, film grain, and entrance-stagger animations that make the UI feel alive.
The shared kit vocabulary (.kit-*) attempting to be the one visual language for all chrome regions.
Node status colors driven from the served registry instead of hardcoded status classes.
Presence dial and chat orb using motion/light rather than text to communicate system state.
Build-intent cards with a lifecycle color language and a demonstrate-first outcome block.
conventions: Token-only styling: almost every color/space/font/size is a `var(--*)` referencing the corpus; literals are avoided or derived via color-mix.
Comments carry design IDs (F1, U2, L10, etc.) and explain why rules exist or what was deleted.
Reduced-motion media query wraps all continuous animations.
Specificity is managed intentionally (e.g., `.kit-sechead .kit-sectitle` to beat `.panel h3`).
concerns: The file is very long and mixes theme, layout, component styling, and animation in one place, which may make it hard to maintain.
Some comments describe deleted rules or retired classes; without seeing the consuming TSX it is unclear whether any stale selectors still exist or whether deletions are complete.
The truncation means the bottom of the file is missing, so the blast-radius/ripple section and anything after it cannot be fully evaluated.
A few rules still embed px literals and font stack literals (e.g., `font-family:"Fraunces",serif`) despite the token-driven convention.
The heavy meta-commentary could drift out of sync with the actual CSS.
notes: A token-aliased, animation-rich global stylesheet that tries to make the canvas app feel like a warm, living command deck while enforcing a shared kit-based visual language.
questions: Which exact file defines the corpus tokens (--acc, --s1, --tx, etc.) and is it imported before this file in main.tsx?
Which React components apply classes like .as-top, .as-rail, .as-canvas, .as-panel?
Is `body.canvas-running` toggled only by useAppController, and does it cover all run states?
Do the node-card status classes (.ran, .cached, .stuck, .failed, .live, .empty) come from the backend render token or from frontend logic?
What is the full content after the truncation point (`.br-ring-circle.br`)?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to act as an interpretive alias+shared-kit layer on top of the corpus design system, and the file largely does that: it aliases legacy variables, defines atmosphere/animation, and provides reusable kit classes for the app chrome.
apparent_themes: Design-token governance and one-source-of-truth for color.
A 'living instrument' aesthetic: motion, light, and atmosphere as status signals.
Shared kit language over bespoke per-region styles.
Demonstrate-first / recognition-by-sight UX for builds and state.
intention_signals: The file is trying to unify the app's look-and-feel by remapping legacy CSS onto a single corpus token set, replacing flat static chrome with a warm, animated, instrument-like deck, and establishing a small reusable kit so every region reads as the same product language.
novelty: The extensive narrative comments that map design decisions, mockup sources, and rule IDs directly onto CSS rules, plus the atmospheric body-layer approach (aurora + grain) that sits behind a transparent tldraw canvas.
contribution: It contributes a token-aliased global theme layer, a set of reusable kit primitives, and a coherent animation/atmosphere system that a fused codebase could keep as the canonical styling vocabulary for the canvas app's chrome and node cards.
summary_for_embedding: A global CSS file for the canvas app that aliases legacy variables to a corpus design-system token set and defines a warm-gold living-instrument atmosphere, shared kit components, HUD panel layouts, node-card states, and lifecycle-colored build-intent surfaces.
intention_for_embedding: To re-skin the entire canvas app from a single corpus token source while giving it a coherent, animated, instrument-deck feel and a reusable shared visual kit.
